title: Absenteeism Crisis Leaves Mwanza Schools Struggling in PSLCE Results
body:
Absenteeism among both teachers and learners has emerged as a major factor behind the poor performance recorded in the Malawi National Examinations Board (MANEB) Standard 8 examinations in Mwanza District.

Director of Education and Sports for the district, Joseph Magombo, disclosed this during an awards ceremony held to recognise outstanding teachers who excelled in the 2025 Primary School Leaving Certificate of Education (PSLCE).

Magombo said persistent absenteeism is disrupting learning in many schools, making it difficult for learners to adequately prepare for national examinations. He further pointed to the lack of adequate teachers' housing as a key challenge contributing to the problem, noting that many teachers struggle to stay close to their duty stations, which affects their consistency in reporting for work and delivering lessons.

The situation is reflected in the district's 2025 PSLCE results, where only one school managed to have two learners selected to government secondary schools, an outcome he described as worrying.

Meanwhile, Joseph Chinkonda was recognised as the district's best-performing teacher. He received a cash prize of K200,000, a trophy, and a chitenje in appreciation of his outstanding performance and dedication to improving education standards.

Education authorities have since emphasised the need for collective efforts to address absenteeism and improve learning conditions in order to raise academic performance across the district.

title: Government Enforces Strict Dress Code for Primary School Teachers
body: The Ministry of Education, Science and Technology has reinforced a strict dress code for primary school teachers, emphasizing professionalism and modesty in line with public service standards. Male teachers are required to wear well-fitting trousers with belts, properly tucked-in shirts, and neckties. Female teachers are expected to dress modestly in dresses or skirts preferably below the knee, or tailored trousers with appropriate blouses. The Ministry stressed that teachers serve as role models to learners and the wider community.

title: Free Education Rollout Receives Mixed Reactions in Malawi
body:
By Mussa Kabichi | 16 March 2026

The government’s rollout of free education in public primary and secondary schools is receiving mixed reactions, with stakeholders welcoming increased access while raising concerns over funding and implementation.

The policy, introduced this term, has eased the financial burden on many households by removing school-related costs such as fees and contributions. At the secondary level, learners selected to government schools can now attend without paying tuition, a development widely praised by parents.

However, findings by Education Matters Online show that while the initiative has boosted enrolment, schools are struggling to cope with limited and delayed funding under the Free Primary Education (FPE) program.

Headteachers and teachers say the current allocation of about K1,000 per learner is insufficient to meet operational demands, particularly for examinations, which in some cases cost up to K2,000 per learner. They also report that funds are often disbursed late and in small portions, affecting the planning and delivery of lessons.

“FPE is a good initiative, but the funding is too little and does not come on time,” said one teacher. Others noted that some schools are failing to print examination papers due to financial constraints, forcing learners to write tests from chalkboards.

Despite these challenges, the policy has led to a noticeable increase in learner participation. Teachers report a rise in the number of pupils sitting for examinations, while many learners say they no longer miss exams due to lack of fees.

“We are happy that we can now sit for exams without paying,” said one learner, though others expressed concern over the difficulty of writing exams from the board due to limited resources.

School committees and other stakeholders have also called for increased and timely funding, emphasizing the need for clear guidelines on resource allocation and management to ensure smooth implementation.

Parents, on the other hand, have largely welcomed the initiative, describing it as a major relief. Many say their children are now able to attend school consistently without the risk of being sent home for non-payment.

While the policy marks a significant step toward expanding access to education, stakeholders say its success will depend on addressing funding gaps and improving implementation. Strengthening financial support and ensuring timely disbursement of resources will be critical to delivering not only access but also quality education across Malawi.

title: Mangochi Education Director Attends Funeral of Primary School Learner
body:
By Mussa Kabichi | March 7, 2026

Director of Education and Sports for Mangochi District, MacDonald Glyn Kuse, is attending the funeral ceremony of Precious Smart, a Standard 7 learner at Mufti Abbas Primary School in Boma Zone.

Precious died on Thursday at Mangochi District Hospital following a short illness, according to sources close to the family.

The funeral ceremony is being held at Issa Mponda Village (Nansenga), located about 20 kilometres from Mangochi Town, where mourners including family members, fellow learners, teachers, and community members have gathered to pay their last respects.

Kuse’s presence at the funeral reflects the education office’s solidarity with the bereaved family and the school community during this period of mourning.

The passing of the learner has left a somber mood among pupils and staff at Mufti Abbas Primary School, highlighting the profound impact of the loss on the school and the wider community.
